deck_title: Japan and South Korea Between U.S. Security Insurance and China Interface
goal: Build an English Slidev deck that explains Northeast Asia's dual-trilateral order through alliance management, with separate Japan and South Korea cases.
note: Outline stage only. Two variants are included for confirmation. No slides.md has been written.

variants.label: Academic-Argument-Heavy
variants.deck_title: Alliance Management in Northeast Asia: Japan and South Korea Between U.S. Security Insurance and China Interface
variants.audience: Instructor and classmates expecting a theory-led argument
variants.goal: Present a structured argument centered on alliance management, abandonment, entrapment, and differentiated Japan/ROK strategies within the 2023-2025 dual-trilateral formation process.

slides.title: Alliance Management in Northeast Asia
slides.intent: Open the deck with the central question and signal that Japan and South Korea will be treated as separate cases.
slides.key_message: The core puzzle is not whether Tokyo and Seoul chose sides, but how they managed two sets of risks at once.

slides.title: The Puzzle: Why Do Two Tracks Coexist?
slides.intent: Frame the empirical puzzle created by the simultaneous rise of U.S.-Japan-ROK security institutionalization and the revival of CJK cooperation.
slides.key_message: From 2023 to 2025, Northeast Asia did not move toward one bloc, but toward two parallel trilateral tracks with different functions.

slides.title: Theoretical Lens: Alliance Management
slides.intent: Define the main theoretical framework before entering the cases.
slides.key_message: Japan and South Korea respond to an alliance dilemma in which abandonment and entrapment must be managed simultaneously.

slides.title: Formation Timeline, 2023-2025
slides.intent: Establish the sequence through which the dual-trilateral structure took shape.
slides.key_message: The dual-track structure emerged through cumulative milestones: Camp David and Washington Declaration on one side, CJK summit and renewed bilateral interfaces on the other.

slides.title: Japan Case I: Why Tokyo Wanted Stronger U.S. Insurance
slides.intent: Explain the strategic logic behind Japan's push for deeper alliance reassurance.
slides.key_message: Tokyo used trilateral and bilateral deterrence mechanisms to reduce abandonment risk under worsening regional security conditions.

slides.title: Japan Case II: Why Tokyo Still Kept a China Interface
slides.intent: Show that stronger balancing did not eliminate the need for crisis management and economic governance channels with China.
slides.key_message: Japan paired stronger deterrence with limited but deliberate interfaces to China through defense and economic mechanisms.

slides.title: Japan Case Synthesis: Balancing-Heavy Alliance Management
slides.intent: Condense the Japanese case into one analytical proposition.
slides.key_message: Japan's strategy is best read as balancing-heavy alliance management: deepen U.S. reassurance, keep China channels narrow but functional.

slides.title: ROK Case I: Why Seoul Wanted Even Stronger Reassurance
slides.intent: Explain why the South Korean case intensified alliance reassurance even more explicitly than Japan.
slides.key_message: Washington Declaration and the NCG made alliance reassurance more visible because Seoul faced sharper nuclear anxiety and credibility concerns.

slides.title: ROK Case II: Why Seoul Still Needed a China Interface
slides.intent: Show how South Korea preserved channels to China despite a pro-alliance turn.
slides.key_message: Because of peninsula dynamics, China leverage over North Korea, and economic exposure, Seoul retained stronger interface incentives than Tokyo.

slides.title: ROK Case Synthesis: Interface-Heavy Alliance Management
slides.intent: Condense the South Korean case into one analytical proposition.
slides.key_message: South Korea also deepened alliance reassurance, but it preserved a thicker China interface because its entrapment and exposure concerns are more immediate.

slides.title: Japan vs. South Korea: Same Logic, Different Weights
slides.intent: Directly compare the two cases under the same theoretical lens.
slides.key_message: Both states manage the same alliance dilemma, but Japan weights balancing more heavily while South Korea weights interface preservation more heavily.

slides.title: What This Means for Northeast Asian Order
slides.intent: Scale the two cases up to a regional-order argument.
slides.key_message: The emerging order is bifurcated by function: deterrence is organized more through U.S.-Japan-ROK, while diplomacy, economic governance, and buffering still need China-facing interfaces.

slides.title: Caveats: This Is Not Pure Hedging and Not a Stable End State
slides.intent: Prevent over-claiming and clarify the limits of the argument.
slides.key_message: The cases should not be reduced to classic hedging, and the dual-trilateral structure remains uneven, contingent, and politically reversible.

slides.title: Conclusion: Two Cases, One Regional Logic
slides.intent: Close the argument and tie the cases back to the thesis.
slides.key_message: Japan and South Korea are not oscillating between camps; they are managing alliance risks through two parallel institutional tracks in a reordered Northeast Asia.

variants.label: Classroom-Presentation-First
variants.deck_title: Why Japan and South Korea Want Both U.S. Security Insurance and a China Interface
variants.audience: Classroom audience that benefits from clear sequencing, lower theory density, and stronger narrative flow
variants.goal: Explain the 2023-2025 formation process in an accessible way while keeping alliance theory as the main interpretive frame and separating Japan and South Korea into two cases.

slides.title: Why Do Japan and South Korea Want Both?
slides.intent: Open with a direct hook that turns the topic into a concrete strategic puzzle.
slides.key_message: The real question is why two U.S. allies strengthened deterrence with Washington while still preserving channels to China.

slides.title: One Region, Two Tracks
slides.intent: Give the audience one visual map of the 2023-2025 process before analysis begins.
slides.key_message: From Camp David to the 2025 CJK and China-facing meetings, Northeast Asia moved into a dual-track structure rather than a single bloc order.

slides.title: Key Idea: Alliance Management, Not Simple Side-Choosing
slides.intent: Introduce the theory in the lightest possible way while keeping it as the main framework.
slides.key_message: Japan and South Korea want stronger U.S. protection against abandonment, but they also want to avoid the costs of entrapment and total rupture with China.

slides.title: Japan Case 1: Why Tokyo Wanted Harder U.S. Insurance
slides.intent: Present Japan's security logic in a clean, classroom-friendly way.
slides.key_message: Tokyo strengthened trilateral and bilateral deterrence because it wanted U.S. commitments to be more institutional, visible, and predictable.

slides.title: Japan Case 2: Why Tokyo Still Kept China Channels Open
slides.intent: Show that the Japan case is not a story of pure containment.
slides.key_message: Even while balancing more strongly, Japan preserved limited China interfaces for crisis management and economic governance.

slides.title: South Korea Case 1: Why Seoul Wanted Even More Reassurance
slides.intent: Explain why the Korean case pushed reassurance further and more visibly.
slides.key_message: Because the nuclear problem is immediate on the peninsula, Seoul sought stronger and more visible reassurance than before through Washington Declaration and the NCG.

slides.title: South Korea Case 2: Why Seoul Also Kept a China Interface
slides.intent: Show why South Korea had stronger incentives than Japan to preserve China-facing channels.
slides.key_message: Seoul still needed high-level communication with China because of North Korea, economic exposure, and the risk of over-locking itself into one confrontation track.

slides.title: Japan and South Korea: Same Dilemma, Different Balance
slides.intent: Give the audience one memorable comparison slide.
slides.key_message: Japan is more balancing-heavy, while South Korea is more interface-heavy, but both are operating under the same alliance-management logic.

slides.title: What This Says About Northeast Asia
slides.intent: Move from the two cases to the regional-order implication.
slides.key_message: Northeast Asia is being reorganized by function: hard security deepens through U.S.-Japan-ROK, while buffering and governance still require China-facing channels.

slides.title: Did the Pattern Stop in 2025?
slides.intent: Use post-2025 evidence as validation without derailing the main 2023-2025 narrative.
slides.key_message: No: later evidence suggests the U.S.-Japan-ROK side kept deepening while the CJK side remained slower but still alive.

slides.title: Final Takeaway
slides.intent: End with the shortest defensible thesis for classroom delivery.
slides.key_message: Japan and South Korea are not choosing between America and China once and for all; they are using two different institutional tracks to manage two different strategic risks.
